---
title: "Task 4 — Project Scoping & Prioritization"
subtitle: "Verasight Data Scientist Case Study"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    embed-resources: true
    theme: litera
    highlight-style: github
---

> **Task 4 (preamble).** "Included below are descriptions of 4 types of projects you could work
> on as a Data Scientist at Verasight. Read through the descriptions and then answer the
> questions below."
>
> *— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4*

# Part 1: Scoping questions

> "For each project, identify the 1-3 of the most important questions you would ask before
> beginning work. Your questions should help you clarify the project scope, the goals of the
> client, assess feasibility, and understand the specific data needs of the project. Questions
> may cover (but are not limited to) sampling frame creation, weighting or analysis
> requirements, deliverables, timelines, or client expectations. Assume you have the resources
> and training you need to complete individual tasks successfully — ask questions to help
> understand the *scope and content* of the projects, not on the mechanics of how to execute
> the tasks themselves."
>
> *— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4, Part 1*

### Project 1: Nationally-representative survey (3,000 US adults)

> "A nationally-representative survey of 3,000 US adults fielded from the Verasight panel.
> Questions were submitted by conference attendees. In addition to the standard deliverables of
> raw data, codebook, and survey weights, this project also requires a public-facing topline &
> crosstab report. Fielding & deliverable preparation should take 2 weeks."
>
> *— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4, Project 1*

::: {.callout-note title="My questions (1–3)"}
1. **The questions submitted by conference attendees** (I'm assuming I'd know the conference from my interactions with the client/sales teams (e.g., academic conference, a specific trade union, etc.)): *The first thing I'd want to know is the type and format of questions that conference attendees have submitted. For example, do they have a coherent theme, how many are there, how many are free-response (which will be harder to analyze)? Are these very technical or are they meant for laity? Who has authority to edit them if necessary and how long might that take (if not me) to get them ready for fielding? And roughly how long will the final questionnaire run -- I'd want a sense of the total length so it's not too much of a burden on respondents and so it's realistic to finalize and field in 2 weeks.*
2. **Cross-tab variables and required demographics:** *A public-facing topline & crosstab report requires a bit more preparation and understanding of the scrutiny they'll face when made public. So I'd be concerned with knowing the most important demographics they require for fielding so I can reserve time to build out the topline & crosstab reports. Also, what are the cross-tab variables? Will they be all the key demographics or only a subset? What are the demographic requirements--e.g., employment status, ethnoracial distribution, political identification, educational attainment, etc.? I'd want to see if there were any hard to reach populations that might be hard to get in time or might create low Ns and therefore be unable to publish on a public-facing topline.*
3. **More details on the public-facing topline & crosstab report:** *A public-facing topline & crosstab report can come under varying levels of scrutiny depending on the intended audience, so I'd like to know who the client expects to read these findings/reports--e.g., news organizations, academic methodologists, the general public? This will also help me know the level of detail to include so it's readable for the intended audience. If the questions are coming from conference participants, it could be commissioned by an organization that might have specific transparency and disclosure requirements, so I'd want to ask more about those. They might also have specific requirement criteria that might not have come up in the demographics conversation (e.g., diversity of individuals with specific disabilities, access to modes of communication, etc.)*
:::

### Project 2: Wyoming voter survey (1,500 voters)

> "A survey of 1,500 voters in Wyoming sampled from voter file records. The sales team notes
> that the client may have demographic quotas. Fielding should take 3 weeks."
>
> *— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4, Project 2*

::: {.callout-note title="My questions (1–3)"}
1. **Follow up on quota samples:** *The note that "the client may have demographic quotas" is a bit vague, so I'd want more clarity on the quotas. Are they pretty standard and easy to fill or will they be more difficult and more granular? Since the sample is coming from a voting file, will there be partisan quotas--in Wyoming it might be much harder to find a 64-year-old college-educated Democrat than their Republican counterpart.*
2. **Voter file:** *I'd want to ask more about the voter file requirements? Who has the file, what is the source? Who will be doing the cleaning (even if they do the cleaning, I'd prob want to do my own quick quality assessment)? Also, how are we getting the sample? This framing implies the client is providing the dataset, so what will we merge it with to get a sample frame? And how do we actually reach these voters -- a voter file gives us records, not a way to contact them, so do we have phones/addresses/emails, or are we matching to a panel?*
3. **What defines a voter:** *"Voter" can mean multiple things: voted in the last federal election, voted in one of the last 4 elections, simply registered, etc. Does the client have any stipulations around this?* 
:::

### Project 3: AI software-developer survey (300 developers)

> "A survey of 300 software developers who program with AI models. The client would like to be
> able to weight the survey results to the population of eligible software developers. The
> timeline is 6 weeks to prepare and 4 weeks to field."
>
> *— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4, Project 3*

::: {.callout-note title="My questions (1–3)"}
1. **Benchmarks and sample frame:** *What benchmarks exist that we could use? There's no Census of software developers who build AI to define the population benchmarks. Perhaps there might be some Stack Overflow software developer survey or a niche BLS survey we could start from? Does the client have a population benchmark in mind already as a starting point? Is the "weight the survey results to the population of eligible software developers" negotiable if we have to weight to a soft target instead (e.g., using a Stack Overflow developer survey that might have problems instead of a more reliable survey like the US Census)? And once we settle on a benchmark, how are we actually sourcing the 300 developers -- do we have enough of them on the Verasight panel or are we recruiting externally, and what's the incidence of finding qualified AI devs?*
2. **Defining eligibility:** *How is the client defining eligibility here--full-time employed, working for specific companies or in specific industries? Are they developing a tool that uses AI or are they training an AI model? Are there regional, demographic, or skill requirements (e.g., background in CS, knows C++ vs Python, has a PhD, etc.)?*
3. **Content of the survey:** *The content of this survey seems not to have been developed -- what will this be about? What kinds of questions will be asked? Who will prepare them? Will they be free response?*
:::

### Project 4: Longitudinal survey of working women (1,000, 4 waves)

> "A longitudinal survey of 1,000 working women. There are 4 waves, each a month apart.
> Fielding should start tomorrow."
>
> *— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4, Project 4*

::: {.callout-note title="My questions (1–3)"}
1. **Retention plan?** *This is a longitudinal survey, meaning we'll want to retain respondents over 4 waves. Retention will be the main concern for this project, so we'll need clarity on the retention plan? How much will you pay/reimburse respondents? If that's not enough, what wiggle room do we have to adjust it (i.e., what's the budget)? What are the re-contact rules (how many times can we follow up with respondents)? Are there specific thresholds for retention? Is the 1,000 the wave 1 or wave 4 goal? What is time between waves? We may need to massively over-recruit to hit our goals.*
2. **"Fielding should start tomorrow."?** *"Fielding should start tomorrow" implies the screener is already proofed, the questionnaire is already done, the sample frame is already pulled, etc. So, my first concern is what "ready" really means here? Are there any adjustments that need to be made, are there any potential issues that we might encounter since we didn't prepare all of the materials? And where is the sample even coming from -- is this the Verasight panel, or a list of working women the client is providing?*
3. **Working women?** *How is working women defined and what are the parameters? Are we looking for full-time or does part-time count? Is there a specific type of job or industry implied by "working" here? What do we do with respondents if their employment status changes during the study?*
:::

# Part 2: Ranking by time intensity

> "Now assume you receive answers to the questions you included above. Given what you know now,
> please rank these projects from 1-4 in order of how time intensive you think they will be for
> you as a Data Scientist. The project you rank first should be the project you expect to take
> the most of your time, and the project you rank 4th should be the project you expect to take
> the least amount of your time. Be explicit about the assumptions you make and provide
> reasoning for the ranking. (Note: There are no right or wrong answers for this question — we
> want to get insight into your thought process.)"
>
> *— 2026 Data Scientist Case Study, Task 4, Part 2*

**My ranking** (1 = most time-intensive, 4 = least):

| Rank | Project |
|:---:|---|
| 1 | Project 3: AI software-developer survey |
| 2 | Project 4: Longitudinal survey of working women |
| 3 | Project 1: Nationally-representative survey |
| 4 | Project 2: Wyoming voter survey |

### 1. Project 3: AI software-developer survey *(most time-intensive)*

I assume the responsibility to develop a sampling frame and benchmarks will be solely ours and constructing a weighting scheme for a population with no frame is open-ended methodological work that will require some time, skill, and creativity. It will require a comprehensive literature review to determine what potential data is out there, a complicated benchmark construction, lots of sensitivity checks, and a defensible writeup that will take time and probably require the eyes of other data scientists. While the timeframe is shorter than Project 4's longitudinal survey, the detailed methodological work on this will require more effort.

If the client has a specific idea of their target sample and a good dataset in mind for benchmarking (and is OK with weighting to soft targets), then this could be an easier lift.

### 2. Project 4: Longitudinal survey of working women

Unless these are rigorous academics who have experience fielding their own surveys, I assume moderate editing to the materials before fielding can begin. I also assume "working women" will be somewhat loosely defined, not specific to any industry or occupation, which will make it easier for fielding. I assume that if someone becomes unemployed during the study, we'll have to drop them from the sample, which will negatively impact retention rates. I also assume that by 1,000 person survey, they meant to start the first wave and that, with waves a month apart, the whole thing wraps in about 3-4 months.

So, the big lift for this project will be retention bc that's not a huge sample for 4 waves where I'd expect a good amount of attrition. We'll need to determine a large initial sample to ensure high enough retention rates and then ensure retention rates are of high enough quality as it proceeds and likely need to adjust incentives if the client has low incentives coming in. If the client has done quality preparation of the materials to the extent they do not need much editing and has a good incentive and retention strategy, that could lighten the expected work on this.

### 3. Project 1: Nationally-representative survey

Normally, I would assume projects using the internal Verasight data to be less demanding than others because of the familiarity with and tidiness of our sample; however, I think the squishiness of the survey questions and public-facing nature of this project make it more difficult than the remaining Wyoming voter survey. I assume most demanding part of this will be the unwieldy nature of the conference participants' submitted questions. These will require organizing and rewriting to improve their precision and quality, many of them will be somewhat technical or jargon-y and will require translation, and I expect there to be a good handful of free response questions. Negotiating the editing of these questions with the client (who I assume will want to retain some level of control over the questions) and the back and forth will require the most effort for me.

I assume the topline & crosstab report and the scrutiny it will receive to be the second biggest time requirement here. I assume the client will come back with mostly generic demographic requirements for the crosstab but have one or two more difficult requirements (maybe a hard to reach demographic or one with low Ns). Since the client is affiliated with a conference, I'll assume there'll be some degree of the intended audience that will have some survey experience and want a more detailed than normal topline & crosstab report, so more time will have to be devoted here than in a normal one. If the client gives me full control over editing their questions and allows me to use my judgment to edit them (as long as I maintain the spirit of the question), that could shave off some of the work here.

### 4. Project 2: Wyoming voter survey *(least time-intensive)*

I assume this will come back as a standard state voter poll with fewer complications--standard demographic quotas and the client wants to define "voter" as anyone who voted in any of the last three elections. The bulk of the work will come from cleaning the voter file and building a sample frame, but the fielding will be straightforward enough -- assuming the client hands us usable contact info (phones/addresses) or a panel match, since reaching voters off a bare file would make it noticeably harder. If I need to obtain and clean the voter file from scratch or if the demographic requirements are more complicated and hard to field, I could see this requiring more effort.
